case_name: The Oriental Insurance Comp. Ltd. ... vs Smt. Rani And Ors. on 12 February, 2019
court: HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT ALLAHABAD, LUCKNOW BENCH

compensation_breakdown.claimant_or_victim: Claimants (wife, two minor children and parents of deceased Ramhet)
compensation_breakdown.interest: 7% per annum from date of petition till actual payment

facts: On 22.06.2009 at about 3:00 p.m. deceased Ramhet was riding Motorcycle No. UP 42-G/2180 from Umaradeeh to Jamo with two pillion riders when Mini Truck No. 44 AH/4081 driven rashly and negligently dashed the motorcycle. Ramhet died on the spot; his father-in-law Shyam Lal and mother-in-law Dhanpata Devi sustained serious injuries. FIR (Crime No. 375/2009) under Sections 338, 279, 427, 304-A IPC was lodged the same day and post-mortem and medical examinations were conducted at District Hospital Sultanpur. The deceased's wife, two minor children and his parents filed Motor Accident Claim Petition No. 232 of 2009 claiming compensation.
arguments_claimant: Claimants argued the deceased was a healthy 32-year-old earning approx. Rs. 5,000 per month from taxi driving and there was no other earning member; they sought Rs.12,62,000/-. They relied on eyewitness evidence and the chargesheet to establish negligent driving by the truck and claimed full compensation from the insurer under Section 166/163A and established principles for dependency calculation.
arguments_respondent: Appellant insurer contended the award was excessive and raised contributory negligence — asserting the deceased was carrying two pillion riders, saw the truck from 25 meters and was negligent. The insurer also challenged procedural aspects (rejection of application under Section 170) and sought to contest liability and quantum in appeal.
ratio_decidendi: The Court held that Tribunals must determine compensation in death cases by following the well settled steps in Smt. Sarla Verma: "Step 1 (Ascertaining the multiplicand) ..."; "Step 2 (Ascertaining the multiplier) ..."; and "Step 3 (Actual calculation) ...". The Court applied those steps to the facts, accepting the Tribunal's notional annual income calculation and choice of multiplier '17' for a 32-year-old to compute loss of dependency and added conventional amounts for funeral, loss of estate and loss of consortium. On insurer liability the Court relied on the principle that "Section 149 casts a duty on the insurer to satisfy judgments and awards against persons insured in respect of third party risks" and that the insurer is liable where insurance coverage was effective at the time of accident. The Court also reaffirmed that the Tribunal's standard is the preponderance of probabilities (not the criminal standard) in motor accident claims and that absence of the driver's examination by insurer/owner does not absolve them when no rebuttal evidence is produced.
final_order: Appeal dismissed and the judgment and award dated 26.04.2011 and order dated 23.03.2011 are confirmed; appellant Oriental Insurance Company Ltd. directed to pay Rs. 4,17,500/- with interest @ 7% p.a. from date of petition till actual payment within one month, failing which MACT to execute recovery proceedings.
summary: This is an appeal by Oriental Insurance Ltd. against a MACT award of Rs.4,17,500/- for death of Ramhet in a collision between a mini truck and a motorcycle on 22.06.2009. The High Court upheld the Tribunal's findings of negligence against the truck driver, applied the Sarla Verma method (multiplier 17 for age 32) for dependency calculation, and confirmed insurer liability because the policy was effective. The appeal was dismissed and the insurer was directed to pay the awarded amount with interest.
embedding_summary: Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Smt. Rani & Ors., High Court of Allahabad (Lucknow Bench), 2019, AIRONLINE 2019 ALL 1546; appeal under Section 173 MV Act against MACT Sultanpur award in MACP No.232/2009 (accident 22.06.2009 at Jamo/Umaradeeh). Collision between Mini Truck No.44 AH/4081 (insured by appellant) and Motorcycle UP 42-G/2180 causing death of driver Ramhet (age ~32) and injuries to pillion riders; FIR under Sections 338, 279, 427, 304-A IPC. Key legal issues: application of Sarla Verma steps and multiplier (multiplier 17) for loss of dependency, insurer's duty under Section 149 MV Act to satisfy awards where insurance cover effective, Section 170 impleading and scope of insurer's defences, and standard of proof (preponderance of probabilities). Court confirmed MACT award of Rs.4,17,500/- with interest @7% p.a., dismissed insurer's appeal and directed payment within one month.
legal_principles: Apply Sarla Verma stepwise method for calculating loss of dependency (ascertain multiplicand, choose multiplier by age, add conventional amounts for loss of estate and consortium)
Choose multiplier from the recognized table with reference to the age of the deceased (multiplier 17 for age 31-35)
Section 149 Motor Vehicles Act casts a duty on insurer to satisfy judgments/awards in respect of third party risks where certificate of insurance was in force
Tribunals in motor accident claims apply preponderance of probabilities standard (not criminal standard of proof beyond reasonable doubt)
Where insurer is impleaded as a party it may contest on all grounds; no requirement to separately move Section 170 when insurer already a party/respondent
Absence of examination of insurer/owner's witnesses is not fatal if no rebuttal evidence produced and prima facie material (FIR, chargesheet, site plan, eyewitnesses) establishes negligence
cases_cited: Smt. Sarla Verma and Others Vs. Delhi Transport Corporation and Another, AIR 2009 Supreme Court 3104
National Insurance Company Limited Vs. Pranay Sethi and Others, AIR 2017 Supreme Court 5157
New India Assurance Company Ltd. Vs. Rula and others, (2000) 3 SCC 195
New India Assurance Company Ltd. Vs. Salma Begum, 2014(4) T.A.C. 630 (All.)
Archit Saini and Another Vs. The Oriental Insurance Company Limited and Others, Civil Appeal No. 7300-7309 of 2016 (order dated 09.02.2018)
Bijoy Kumar Dutta Vs. Bidyadhar Dutta and Others, 2006(1) T.A.C. 969 (S.C.)
Mangla Ram Vs. The Oriental Insurance Company Ltd. And Others, Civil Appeal Nos. 2499-2500 of 2018
United India Insurance Co. Ltd. Vs. Shila Datta, (2011) 10 SCC 509
N.K.V. Bros. (P) Ltd. Vs. M. Karumai Ammal and Ors., (1980) 3 SCC 457
bm25_text: The Oriental Insurance Comp. Ltd. ... vs Smt. Rani And Ors. on 12 February, 2019 HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT ALLAHABAD, LUCKNOW BENCH 2019 On 22.06.2009 at about 3:00 p.m. deceased Ramhet was riding Motorcycle No. UP 42-G/2180 from Umaradeeh to Jamo with two pillion riders when Mini Truck No. 44 AH/4081 driven rashly and negligently dashed the motorcycle. Ramhet died on the spot; his father-in-law Shyam Lal and mother-in-law Dhanpata Devi sustained serious injuries. FIR (Crime No. 375/2009) under Sections 338, 279, 427, 304-A IPC was lodged the same day and post-mortem and medical examinations were conducted at District Hospital Sultanpur. The deceased's wife, two minor children and his parents filed Motor Accident Claim Petition No. 232 of 2009 claiming compensation. Claimants argued the deceased was a healthy 32-year-old earning approx. Rs. 5,000 per month from taxi driving and there was no other earning member; they sought Rs.12,62,000/-. They relied on eyewitness evidence and the chargesheet to establish negligent driving by the truck and claimed full compensation from the insurer under Section 166/163A and established principles for dependency calculation. Appellant insurer contended the award was excessive and raised contributory negligence — asserting the deceased was carrying two pillion riders, saw the truck from 25 meters and was negligent. The insurer also challenged procedural aspects (rejection of application under Section 170) and sought to contest liability and quantum in appeal. The Court held that Tribunals must determine compensation in death cases by following the well settled steps in Smt. Sarla Verma: "Step 1 (Ascertaining the multiplicand) ..."; "Step 2 (Ascertaining the multiplier) ..."; and "Step 3 (Actual calculation) ...". The Court applied those steps to the facts, accepting the Tribunal's notional annual income calculation and choice of multiplier '17' for a 32-year-old to compute loss of dependency and added conventional amounts for funeral, loss of estate and loss of consortium. On insurer liability the Court relied on the principle that "Section 149 casts a duty on the insurer to satisfy judgments and awards against persons insured in respect of third party risks" and that the insurer is liable where insurance coverage was effective at the time of accident. The Court also reaffirmed that the Tribunal's standard is the preponderance of probabilities (not the criminal standard) in motor accident claims and that absence of the driver's examination by insurer/owner does not absolve them when no rebuttal evidence is produced. Appeal dismissed and the judgment and award dated 26.04.2011 and order dated 23.03.2011 are confirmed; appellant Oriental Insurance Company Ltd. directed to pay Rs. 4,17,500/- with interest @ 7% p.a. from date of petition till actual payment within one month, failing which MACT to execute recovery proceedings. This is an appeal by Oriental Insurance Ltd. against a MACT award of Rs.4,17,500/- for death of Ramhet in a collision between a mini truck and a motorcycle on 22.06.2009. The High Court upheld the Tribunal's findings of negligence against the truck driver, applied the Sarla Verma method (multiplier 17 for age 32) for dependency calculation, and confirmed insurer liability because the policy was effective. The appeal was dismissed and the insurer was directed to pay the awarded amount with interest. Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Smt. Court confirmed MACT award of Rs.4,17,500/- with interest @7% p.a., dismissed insurer's appeal and directed payment within one month.
